name: coorx

metadata.name: coorx
metadata.summary: Object-oriented coordinate system transforms in pure Python

documentation:
Coorx
==========

Object-oriented linear and nonlinear coordinate system transforms.

* A collection of different types of coordinate system transform classes with unit test coverage
* Easy methods for mapping coordinate data through these transforms
* Transform composition and simplification
* Transforms intelligently map data types including numpy arrays, lists, etc.

Wishlist:

* Automatic generation of composite transforms from a coordinate system graph
* Coordinate arrays that know which coordinate system they live in to handle automatic mapping
* Conversion of transforms between ITK, Qt, scikit-image, vispy, etc.
* Numba, cuda optimization


Installation
============

To install the package from PyPI, use the following command:

```
pip install coorx
```

Usage
=====

Scale and translate 2D coordinates:

```python
import numpy as np
from coorx import STTransform

coords = np.array([
    [ 0,  0],
    [ 1,  2],
    [20, 21],
])

tr = STTransform(scale=(10, 1), offset=(5, 5))

print(tr.map(coords))
```

Compose multiple transforms together:

```
import numpy as np
from coorx import STTransform, AffineTransform, CompositeTransform

coords = np.array([
    [0, 0, 0],
    [1, 2, 3],
    [-10, -200, -3000],
])

tr1 = STTransform(scale=(1, 10, 100))

tr2 = AffineTransform(dims=3)
tr2.rotate(90, axis=(0, 0, 1))

tr3 = CompositeTransform([tr2, tr1])

print(tr3.map(coords))
```

Todo
====

* import bilinear, SRT transforms from pyqtgraph
* import coordinate system graph handling from vispy
* make coordinate system dimensionality explicit
* unit tests against ITK output


Credit
======

Coorx is adapted from code originally written for VisPy (vispy.org),
inspired by the nice transform classes in ITK, and
maintained by the Allen Institute for Brain Science.

functions.name: coorx.systems.get_coordinate_system
documentation: Return a CoordinateSystem instance.

classes.name: coorx.systems.CoordinateSystemGraph

methods.name: coorx.systems.CoordinateSystemGraph.check_system
documentation:
Check that a system exists with the given name and ndim.

If `create` is True, then the system may be created if it does not already exist.

Return the named CoordinateSystem instance.

methods.name: coorx.systems.CoordinateSystemGraph.system
documentation: Return a CoordinateSystem belonging to this graph.

methods.name: coorx.systems.CoordinateSystemGraph.transform
documentation: Return the transform linking cs1 to cs2, or raise KeyError if none is defined.

Multiple coordinate systems connected by transforms.

A CoordinateSystemGraph keeps track of the relationships between many coordinate systems.
This makes it possible to automatically determine a chain of transforms that map
between any two coordinate systems (as long as they are indirectly connected within
the graph). 

CoordinateSystemGraph also provides basic sanity checking:
- Only one coordinate system of each name, and that it has the expected properties
  (such as dimensionality)
- Only one transform connecting any pair of coordinate systems
  (requires unique_transforms=True; the default graph ignores this constraint)

classes.name: coorx._util.TransformCache

methods.name: coorx._util.TransformCache.get
documentation:
Get a transform from the cache that maps along *path*, which must
be a list of Transforms to apply in reverse order (last transform is
applied first).

Accessed items have their age reset to 0.

methods.name: coorx._util.TransformCache.roll
documentation:
Increase the age of all items in the cache by 1. Items whose age
is greater than self.max_age will be removed from the cache.

Utility class for managing a cache of ChainTransforms.

This is an LRU cache; items are removed if they are not accessed after
*max_age* calls to roll().

Notes
-----
This class is used by SceneCanvas to ensure that ChainTransform instances
are re-used across calls to draw_visual(). SceneCanvas creates one
TransformCache instance for each top-level visual drawn, and calls
roll() on each cache before drawing, which removes from the cache any
transforms that were not accessed during the last draw cycle.

classes.name: coorx.util.AxisSelectionEmbeddedTransform
documentation:
Wraps any transform in a larger-dimensional transform that passes specific axes to the wrapped
transform, while keeping other axes unchanged.

For example, you could use a 2D transform to affect just the x,y axes in a 3D space.

classes.name: coorx.util.HomogeneousEmbeddedTransform
documentation:
Wraps any transform that uses homogeneous coordinates, 
allowing to operate with nonhomogeneous inputs/outputs instead.

classes.name: coorx.nonlinear.LogTransform

methods.name: coorx.nonlinear.LogTransform.base
documentation:
*base* is a tuple containing the log base values that should be
applied to each axis of the input vector. If any axis has a base == 0,
then that axis is not affected.

ND transform perfoming logarithmic transformation.

Maps (x, y, z) => (log(base_x, x), log(base_y, y), log(base_z, z))

No transformation is applied for axes with base == 0.

If base < 0, then the inverse function is applied: x => base.x ** x

Parameters
----------
base : array-like
    Base values for each axis; length must be the same as the dimensionality of the transform. 
    A base value of 0 disables the transform for that axis.

classes.name: coorx.nonlinear.PolarTransform
documentation:
Polar transform

Maps (theta, r, z) to (x, y, z), where `x = r*cos(theta)`
and `y = r*sin(theta)`.

classes.name: coorx.nonlinear.LensDistortionTransform
documentation:
https://docs.opencv.org/4.x/dc/dbb/tutorial_py_calibration.html

Coefficients are (k1, k2, p1, p2, k3)
Where k1, k2, and k3 are radial distortion (coordinates are multiplied by 1 + k1*r^2 + k2*r^4 + k3*r^6),
and p1, p2 are tangential distortion coefficients.

classes.name: coorx.coordinates.PointArray

methods.name: coorx.coordinates.PointArray._interpret_input
documentation:
Interpret initial coordinates argument, return a numerical array of coordinates 
and a coordinate system if it was present in the input.

Represents an N-dimensional array of points. 

Although the array is instantiated with the coordinates of these points in a particular
coordinate system, one may request the coordinates mapped to any other system.

Parameters
----------
coordinates : array-like
    Array or list of coordinates. These may be numerical or Point instances.
system : str | System | None
    The coordinate system to which th points in *coordinates* belong.
graph : str | CoordinateSystemGraph
    If *system* is a string, then the coordinate system is looked up from this graph.

classes.name: coorx.coordinates.Point
documentation:
Represents a single point in space; one may request the coordinates of this point 
in any coordinate system.

modules.name: coorx.matrices
documentation: Library of matrix transformation functions adapted from vispy.

functions.name: coorx.matrices.translate
documentation:
Return an N-dimensional translation matrix (of shape N+1,N+1)

Parameters
----------
offset : array-like
    Amount to translate each axis
dtype : dtype | None
    Output type (if None, then same as *offset*).

Returns
-------
M : ndarray
    Transformation matrix describing the translation.

functions.name: coorx.matrices.scale
documentation:
Return an N-dimensional scaling matrix (of shape N+1,N+1)

Parameters
----------
scale : array-like, shape (3,)
    Scale factors for each axis
dtype : dtype | None
    Output type (if None, then same as *scale*).

Returns
-------
M : ndarray
    Transformation matrix describing the scaling.

documentation:

dtype : dtype | None

functions.name: coorx.matrices.rotate3d
documentation:
Return a 3D rotation matrix (shape 3,3) for rotation about a vector.

Parameters
----------
angle : float

axis : ndarray
    The x, y, z coordinates of the axis direction vector.
dtype : dtype | None

functions.name: coorx.matrices.affine_map
documentation:
Find an N-D affine transformation that maps points1 onto points2.

Arguments are specified as arrays of coordinates, shape (N+1, N).

functions.name: coorx.matrices.bilinear_map2d
documentation:
Find a bilinear transformation matrix (shape 2x4) that maps 2D points1 onto 2D points2.

# Parameters

points1

: Array of N+1 2D points

points2

: Array of N+1 2D points

To use this matrix to map a point \[x,y\]:

```
mapped = np.dot(matrix, [x*y, x, y, 1])
```

functions.name: coorx.matrices.frustum
documentation:
Create view frustum

Parameters
----------
left : float

right : float
    Right coordinate of the field of view.
bottom : float
    Bottom coordinate of the field of view.
top : float
    Top coordinate of the field of view.
znear : float
    Near coordinate of the field of view.
zfar : float
    Far coordinate of the field of view.

Returns
-------
M : ndarray
    View frustum matrix (4x4).

modules.name: coorx.composite

Copyright (c) Vispy Development Team. All Rights Reserved.
Distributed under the (new) BSD License. See vispy/LICENSE.txt for more info.

classes.name: coorx.composite.CompositeTransform

methods.name: coorx.composite.CompositeTransform.transforms
documentation:
The list of transform that make up the transform chain.

The order of transforms is given such that the first transform in the
list is the first to be invoked when mapping coordinates through
the chain.

For example, the following two mappings are equivalent:

```
# Map coordinates through individual transforms:
trans1 = STTransform(scale=(2, 3), translate=(0, 1))
trans2 = PolarTransform()
mapped = trans2.map(trans1.map(coords))

# Equivalent mapping through chain:
chain = CompositeTransform([trans1, trans2])
mapped = chain.map(coords)
```

methods.name: coorx.composite.CompositeTransform.simplified
documentation: A simplified representation of the same transformation.

modules.name: coorx.base_transform
documentation:
API Issues to work out:

  - MatrixTransform and STTransform both have 'scale' and 'translate'
    attributes, but they are used in very different ways. It would be nice
    to keep this consistent, but how?

  - Need a transform.map_rect function that returns the bounding rectangle of
    a rect after transformation. Non-linear transforms might need to work
    harder at this, but we can provide a default implementation that
    works by mapping a selection of points across a grid within the original
    rect.
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-
Copyright (c) Vispy Development Team. All Rights Reserved.
Distributed under the (new) BSD License. See vispy/LICENSE.txt for more info.

classes.name: coorx.base_transform.BaseTransform

methods.name: coorx.base_transform.BaseTransform.dims
documentation: Tuple holding the (input, output) dimensions for this transform.

methods.name: coorx.base_transform.BaseTransform._dims_from_params
documentation:
Determine dimensionality from parameters.

If *dims* is provided, then it must be a tuple (in, out) and it must agree with the length of all provided parameters.
If *dims* is not provided, then it is determined from length of parameters, which must be in agreement.

methods.name: coorx.base_transform.BaseTransform.systems
documentation: The CoordinateSystem instances mapped from and to by this transform.

methods.name: coorx.base_transform.BaseTransform.map
documentation:
Return *obj* mapped through the forward transformation.

Parameters
----------
    obj : tuple (x,y) or (x,y,z)
          array with shape (..., 2) or (..., 3)

methods.name: coorx.base_transform.BaseTransform._map
documentation:
Map a 2D array (n_pts, n_dims) through this transform.

This method must be redefined in sublcasses.

methods.name: coorx.base_transform.BaseTransform.imap
documentation:
Return *obj* mapped through the inverse transformation.

Parameters
----------
    obj : tuple (x,y) or (x,y,z)
          array with shape (..., 2) or (..., 3)

methods.name: coorx.base_transform.BaseTransform._imap
documentation:
Map a 2D array (n_pts, n_dims) through the inverse of this transform.

This method may be redefined in sublcasses.

methods.name: coorx.base_transform.BaseTransform._prepare_and_map
documentation:
Convert a mappable object to a 2D numpy array, pass it through this Transform's _map method, 
then convert and return the result. 

The Transform's _map method will be called with a 2D array
of shape (N, M), where N is the number of points and M is the number of dimensions. 
Accepts lists, tuples, and arrays of any dimensionality and flattens extra dimensions into N.
After mapping, any flattened axes are re-expanded to match the original input shape.

For list, tuple, and array inputs, the return value is a numpy array of the same shape as 
the input, with the exception that the last dimension is determined only by the return value.

Alternatively, any class may determine how to map itself by defining a _coorx_transform() 
method that accepts this transform as an argument.

methods.name: coorx.base_transform.BaseTransform._prepare_arg_for_mapping
documentation:
Convert arg to a 2D numpy array.

If the argument ndim is > 2, then all dimensions except the last are flattened.

Return the reshaped array and a tuple containing the original shape.

methods.name: coorx.base_transform.BaseTransform._restore_shape
documentation: Return an array with shape determined by shape[:-1] + (arg.shape[-1],)

methods.name: coorx.base_transform.BaseTransform.inverse
documentation: The inverse of this transform.

methods.name: coorx.base_transform.BaseTransform.dynamic
documentation:
Boolean flag that indicates whether this transform is expected to
change frequently.

Transforms that are flagged as dynamic will not be collapsed in
`ChainTransform.simplified`. This allows changes to the transform
to propagate through the chain without requiring the chain to be
re-simplified.

methods.name: coorx.base_transform.BaseTransform.params
documentation: Return a dict of parameters specifying this transform.

methods.name: coorx.base_transform.BaseTransform.set_params
documentation:
Set parameters specifying this transform.

Parameter names must be the same as the keys in self.params.

methods.name: coorx.base_transform.BaseTransform.save_state
documentation: Return serializable parameters that specify this transform.

methods.name: coorx.base_transform.BaseTransform.to_vispy
documentation: Return a VisPy transform that is equivalent to this transform.

methods.name: coorx.base_transform.BaseTransform._update
documentation: Called to inform any listeners that this transform has changed.

methods.name: coorx.base_transform.BaseTransform.__mul__
documentation:
Transform multiplication returns a new transform that is equivalent to
the two operands performed in series.

By default, multiplying two Transforms `A * B` will return
ChainTransform([A, B]). Subclasses may redefine this operation to
return more optimized results.

To ensure that both operands have a chance to simplify the operation,
all subclasses should follow the same procedure. For `A * B`:

1. A.__mul__(B) attempts to generate an optimized transform product.
2. If that fails, it must:

       * return super(A).__mul__(B) OR
       * return NotImplemented if the superclass would return an
         invalid result.

3. When BaseTransform.__mul__(A, B) is called, it returns 
   NotImplemented, which causes B.__rmul__(A) to be invoked.
4. B.__rmul__(A) attempts to generate an optimized transform product.
5. If that fails, it must:

       * return super(B).__rmul__(A) OR
       * return ChainTransform([B, A]) if the superclass would return
         an invalid result.

6. When BaseTransform.__rmul__(B, A) is called, ChainTransform([A, B])
   is returned.

BaseTransform is a base class that defines a pair of complementary
coordinate mapping functions in both python and GLSL.

All BaseTransform subclasses define map() and imap() methods that map
an object through the forward or inverse transformation, respectively.

Optionally, an inverse() method returns a new transform performing the
inverse mapping.

Note that although all classes should define both map() and imap(), it
is not necessarily the case that imap(map(x)) == x; there may be instances
where the inverse mapping is ambiguous or otherwise meaningless.

methods.name: coorx.base_transform.ChangeEvent.sources
documentation: A list of all transforms that changed leading to this event

classes.name: coorx.linear.NullTransform

methods.name: coorx.linear.NullTransform._map

Return the input array unmodified.

methods.name: coorx.linear.NullTransform._imap

Return the input array unmodified.

Parameters

Transform having no effect on coordinates (identity transform).

classes.name: coorx.linear.TTransform

methods.name: coorx.linear.TTransform._map
documentation:
Return translated coordinates.

Parameters
----------
coords : array-like
    Coordinates to map. The length of the last array dimenstion
    must be equal to the input dimensionality of the transform.

Returns
-------
coords : ndarray
    Mapped coordinates: coords + translation

methods.name: coorx.linear.TTransform._imap
documentation:
Return inverse-mapped coordinates.

Parameters
----------
coords : array-like
    Coordinates to inverse map. The length of the last array dimenstion
    must be equal to the input dimensionality of the transform.

Returns
-------
coords : ndarray
    Mapped coordinates: coords - translation

methods.name: coorx.linear.TTransform.translate
documentation:
Change the translation of this transform by the amount given.

Parameters
----------
offset : array-like
    The values to be added to the current translation of the transform.

Transform performing only translation.

Input/output dimensionality of this transform may be set by the length of the offset parameter.

Parameters
----------
offset : array-like
    Translation distances.
dims : tuple
    (input, output) dimensions for transform.

classes.name: coorx.linear.STTransform

methods.name: coorx.linear.STTransform._map
documentation:
Return coordinates mapped by scale and translation.

Parameters
----------
coords : array-like
    Coordinates to map. The length of the last array dimenstion
    must be equal to the input dimensionality of the transform.

Returns
-------
coords : ndarray
    Mapped coordinates: coords * scale + offset

methods.name: coorx.linear.STTransform._imap
documentation:
Return coordinates inverse-mapped by translation and scale.

Parameters
----------
coords : array-like
    Coordinates to inverse map. The length of the last array dimenstion
    must be equal to the input dimensionality of the transform.

Returns
-------
coords : ndarray
    Mapped coordinates: (coords - offset) / scale

methods.name: coorx.linear.STTransform.translate
documentation:
Change the translation of this transform by the amount given.

Parameters
----------
offset : array-like
    The values to be added to the current translation of the transform.

methods.name: coorx.linear.STTransform.zoom
documentation:
Update the transform such that its scale factor is changed, but
the specified center point is left unchanged.

Parameters
----------
zoom : array-like
    Values to multiply the transform's current scale
    factors.
center : array-like
    The center point around which the scaling will take place.
mapped : bool
    Whether *center* is expressed in mapped coordinates (True) or
    unmapped coordinates (False).

methods.name: coorx.linear.STTransform.from_mapping
documentation:
Create an STTransform from the given mapping

See `set_mapping` for details.

Parameters
----------
x0 : array-like
    Start.
x1 : array-like
    End.

Returns
-------
t : instance of STTransform
    The transform.

methods.name: coorx.linear.STTransform.set_mapping
documentation:
Configure this transform such that it maps points x0 onto x1

# Parameters

x0

: Two source points

x1

: Two destination points

# Examples

For example, if we wish to map the corners of a rectangle:

```
>>> p1 = [[0, 0], [200, 300]]
```

onto a unit cube:

```
>>> p2 = [[-1, -1], [1, 1]]
```

then we can generate the transform as follows:

```
>>> tr = STTransform()
>>> tr.set_mapping(p1, p2)
>>> assert tr.map(p1)[:,:2] == p2  # test
```

Transform performing only scale and translate, in that order.

Input/output dimensionality of this transform may be set by the length of the scale or offset parameters.

Parameters
----------
scale : array-like
    Scale factors.
offset : array-like
    Translation distances.

classes.name: coorx.linear.AffineTransform

methods.name: coorx.linear.AffineTransform._map
documentation:
Map coordinates

Parameters
----------
coords : array-like
    Coordinates to map. The length of the last array dimenstion
    must be equal to the input dimensionality of the transform.

Returns
-------
coords : ndarray
    Mapped coordinates: (M * coords) + offset

methods.name: coorx.linear.AffineTransform._imap
documentation:
Inverse map coordinates

Parameters
----------
coords : array-like
    Coordinates to inverse map. The length of the last array dimenstion
    must be equal to the input dimensionality of the transform.

Returns
-------
coords : ndarray
    Mapped coordinates: M_inv * (coords - offset)

methods.name: coorx.linear.AffineTransform.full_matrix
documentation:
Return a matrix of shape (N+1, N+1) that contains both self.matrix
and self.offset:

```
[[m11, m21, m31, o1],
 [m21, m22, m32, o2],
 [m31, m32, m33, o3],
 [  0,   0,   0,  1]]
```

The full matrix can be multiplied by other similar matrices in order to compose affine
transforms together.

methods.name: coorx.linear.AffineTransform.translate
documentation:
Add to the offset.

The translation is applied *after* the transformations already present.

Parameters
----------
pos : arrayndarray
    Position to translate by.

methods.name: coorx.linear.AffineTransform.scale
documentation:
Scale the matrix about a given origin.

The scaling is applied *after* the transformations already present
in the matrix.

Parameters
----------
scale : array-like
    Scale factors along x, y and z axes.
center : array-like or None
    The x, y and z coordinates to scale around. If None,
    (0, 0, 0) will be used.

methods.name: coorx.linear.AffineTransform.rotate
documentation:
Rotate the matrix by some angle about a given axis.

The rotation is applied *after* the transformations already present
in the matrix.

Parameters
----------
angle : float
    The angle of rotation in degrees.
axis : array-like or None
    The x, y and z coordinates of the axis vector to rotate around (only for 3D).

methods.name: coorx.linear.AffineTransform.set_mapping
documentation:
Set to a transformation matrix that maps points1 onto points2.

Parameters
----------
points1 : array-like, shape (4, 3)
    Four starting coordinates.
points2 : array-like, shape (4, 3)
    Four ending coordinates.

methods.name: coorx.linear.AffineTransform.reset
documentation: Reset this transform to have an identity matrix and no offset.

Affine transformation class

Parameters
----------
matrix : array-like | None
    Array to use for the transform. If None, then an identity transform is
    assumed. The shape of the matrix determines the (output, input)
    dimensions of the transform.
offset : array-like | None
    The translation to apply in this affine transform.
dims : tuple
    Optionally specifies the (input, output) dimensions of this transform.

documentation:
Set this transform based on the elements of *m*
The input matrix must be affine AND have no shear,
otherwise the conversion will most likely fail.

methods.name: coorx.linear.SRT3DTransform.as2D
documentation: Return an SRT2DTransform representing the x,y portion of this transform (if possible)

methods.name: coorx.linear.SRT3DTransform.set_mapping
documentation:
Set to a transformation that maps points1 onto points2.

Parameters
----------
points1 : ndarray, shape (N, 3)
    Input coordinates.
points2 : ndarray, shape (N, 3)
    Output coordinates.

Transform implemented as 4x4 affine that can always be represented as a combination of 3 matrices: scale * rotate * translate
This transform has no shear; angles are always preserved.

methods.name: coorx.linear.PerspectiveTransform.set_frustum
documentation: Set the frustum

3D perspective or orthographic matrix transform using homogeneous coordinates.

Assumes a camera at the origin, looking toward the -Z axis.
The camera's top points toward +Y, and right points toward +X.

Points inside the perspective frustum are mapped to the range [-1, +1] along all three axes.
